**Bruno Fortichiari** (8 February 1892[[1]](#cite_note-1) – 4 January 1981) was an Italian [politician](/wiki/Politician "Politician") and [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") [revolutionary](/wiki/Revolutionary "Revolutionary"). He was among the founders of the [Communist Party of Italy (PCd'I)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") in 1921.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bruno_Fortichiari&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Fortichiari was born in [Luzzara](/wiki/Luzzara "Luzzara"), in the Italian region of [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna"). He began his activity as a journalist and [socialist activist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") at an early age, and, in the early 1910s, he met [Amadeo Bordiga](/wiki/Amadeo_Bordiga "Amadeo Bordiga"). In December 1912, he was appointed responsible for the [Milanese](/wiki/Milan "Milan") section of the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party"). He was the author of the order that expelled the pro-[National Syndicalist](/wiki/National_Syndicalism "National Syndicalism") and future [Fascist](/wiki/Fascism_in_Italy "Fascism in Italy") ruler [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") from the party in 1914, and, in contrast to Mussolini, was among the foremost opponents in first line to protest against the Italian intervention in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

After the war, he was instrumental in all the events that led to the founding of the PCd'I in [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") (21 January 1921). He was a member of the executive committee of the new grouping, together with Bordiga, [Ruggero Grieco](/wiki/Ruggero_Grieco "Ruggero Grieco"), [Luigi Repossi](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Repossi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luigi Repossi (page does not exist)") and [Umberto Terracini](/wiki/Umberto_Terracini "Umberto Terracini"), with responsibility for the illegal activities against the increasing success of Fascist forces in repressing [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") forces. He was elected to the [Italian Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Italian_Chamber_of_Deputies "Italian Chamber of Deputies") in 1924.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

When all the Italian Communist deputies were arrested (8 November 1926), Fortichiari was sentenced to five years of [internment](/wiki/Internment "Internment"), but he was released after one year for being affected by [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"). He moved to Milan, but here he met difficulties in finding a regular job, which were aggravated by his expulsion from the PCI in 1929.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), he came in contact with members of the [Internationalist Communist Party](/wiki/Internationalist_Communist_Party_(Italy) "Internationalist Communist Party (Italy)"), a group led by ex-PCI members who disagreed with the moderate politics of the party in opposition to [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") demands. Despite the PCI's campaign against him, Fortichiari was readmitted to its ranks after the war, although he held secondary positions and was under a strict control of the party [cadres](/wiki/Professional_revolutionaries "Professional revolutionaries"), who were arguably weary of his intact charisma as the main figure inside an anti-[Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") current.

In 1956, he was again expelled from the PCI. He remained active until his death, apart from a hiatus in 1965-1970, trying to collect the dissension against the party's official doctrine in the [communist left](/wiki/Communist_left "Communist left") movement.

Fortichiari died in Milan at the age of 88.
